---
title: "Messy real-world data"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 14
difficulty: "intermediate"
prereqs:
  - "M3 Ch4 — Cleaning data"
learning_objectives:
  - "Normalise inconsistent text by fixing case and whitespace"
  - "Parse numbers hidden in strings — currency symbols, commas, and units"
  - "Consolidate categories that mean the same thing into one label"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 18
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
review_cards:
  - id: case-and-whitespace
    q: "Why do '  Male', 'male', and 'M' count as three different values to pandas?"
    a: "Pandas compares text exactly, character for character. Different capitalisation, stray spaces, and abbreviations all produce distinct strings, so a count or a group-by treats them as separate categories even though a human reads them as one."
  - id: normalise-text
    q: "How do you normalise a text column's case and whitespace?"
    a: "Use the vectorised string methods on the column: .str.strip() removes leading and trailing spaces, and .str.lower() forces one case. Chaining them — .str.strip().str.lower() — collapses spacing and case differences in one pass."
  - id: number-in-a-string
    q: "Why can't you do maths on a column like '₹1,200', and how do you fix it?"
    a: "It is text, not a number — the currency symbol and the thousands comma make it a string, so sums and means fail or concatenate. Strip the non-numeric characters with .str.replace(...), then convert with .astype(float)."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=intermediate >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

You ask a hundred people for their gender on a paper form and type the answers
into a spreadsheet. Back come `"Male"`, `"male"`, `"M"`, `" Male "` with a stray
space, and `"MALE"` in a fit of enthusiasm. To you they all mean the same
thing. To pandas they are **five different values** — because the computer compares
text exactly, character for character, and a capital `M` is not a lowercase `m`.

That gap between what a human reads and what a machine stores is the daily reality
of real-world data. The same trouble shows up everywhere:

- **Inconsistent text** — `"Mumbai"`, `"mumbai "`, `"MUMBAI"` are one city to you,
  three categories to a `value_counts()`.
- **Numbers wearing a costume** — `"₹1,200"` looks like a price, but the currency
  symbol and the comma make it *text*, and you can't add text.
- **The same idea, many spellings** — `"M"`, `"male"`, and `"Male"` all mean one
  category that you want counted once.

In Chapter 4 you handled *missing* values and wrong *types*: holes in the table and
columns stored as the wrong dtype. This chapter is about the rows that are present
and look fine but are written **inconsistently** — the messy strings and formats
that quietly split one real category into many and hide numbers inside text. The
fixes are a small, dependable set of pandas **string methods**.

> You'll need from before: counting and repairing columns (M3 Ch4). Here we repair
> a different kind of mess — not the gaps, but the formatting.

---

## Text the computer sees, not text you read

Start with the gender column from the hook. Put it in a Series and ask
`value_counts()` how many of each value there are.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Six entries that mean two things — but pandas counts six near-distinct values"
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.Series(["  Male", "male", "M", "Female ", "FEMALE", "f"])
raw.value_counts()
```

Every entry comes back as its **own** category. `"  Male"` (with leading spaces) and
`"male"` are different; `"Female "` (trailing space) and `"FEMALE"` are different.
Two real groups — male and female — have shattered into six. Any analysis built on
this column is wrong before it starts: a group-by would make six groups, a chart
six bars, a model six categories where there are two.

Two differences cause almost all of this:

- **Whitespace** — invisible spaces at the start or end. `"Male"` and `"Male "`
  look identical on screen but are not equal.
- **Case** — `"Male"`, `"male"`, and `"MALE"` differ only in capitalisation.

Pandas gives you a vectorised string toolkit under the `.str` accessor that fixes
both across a whole column at once — no loop. `.str.strip()` removes leading and
trailing whitespace; `.str.lower()` forces everything to lowercase. Chain them.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Strip whitespace and lowercase the whole column in one pass"
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.Series(["  Male", "male", "M", "Female ", "FEMALE", "f"])
tidied = raw.str.strip().str.lower()
tidied.value_counts()
```

The six categories have already collapsed toward three: `"male"`, `"female"`, and
the stragglers `"m"` and `"f"`. Stripping and lowercasing did the heavy lifting —
`"  Male"`, `"male"`, and `"MALE"` are now the single string `"male"`. What remains
is a question of *meaning*, not formatting: `"m"` and `"f"` are abbreviations that
stand for the full words. That is the next job.

## Consolidating categories that mean the same thing

`.str.strip().str.lower()` fixes how text is *written*. It cannot know that `"m"`
*means* `"male"` — that is domain knowledge only you have. To fold synonyms and
abbreviations into one label, write the mapping down explicitly and apply it with
`.replace()`, which swaps any value it finds as a dictionary key for the
corresponding value.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Map abbreviations onto full labels so each category is counted once"
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.Series(["  Male", "male", "M", "Female ", "FEMALE", "f"])
tidied = raw.str.strip().str.lower()

mapping = {"m": "male", "f": "female"}
consolidated = tidied.replace(mapping)
consolidated.value_counts()
```

Now there are exactly **two** categories: `male` with 3 and `female` with 3. Notice
the order of operations — strip and lowercase *first*, then map. If you tried to map
the raw values you would need keys for `"  Male"`, `"MALE"`, `"Male "` and every
other variant; by normalising case and spacing first, your mapping only has to know
the *clean* spellings (`"m"`, `"f"`) it still needs to fold in. A small, readable
dictionary instead of a sprawling one.

This pattern — **normalise, then map** — is how you turn a column that a survey,
a web form, or three different data-entry clerks filled in into the tidy set of
categories your analysis expects.

## Numbers hiding inside strings

The other classic mess is a number that arrives dressed as text. Prices, amounts,
and measurements often carry a symbol or a separator: `"₹1,200"`, `"$3.50"`,
`"1,050 kg"`. To a person these are clearly quantities. To pandas the currency
symbol, the thousands comma, and the unit are ordinary characters, so the whole
value is a **string** — and string columns refuse to do arithmetic. Try to sum them
and pandas either errors or, worse, glues the texts end to end.

The fix is two steps: **strip the non-numeric characters**, then **convert** what
remains to a real number with `.astype(float)`.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Strip the currency symbol and comma, then convert the column to numbers"
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.DataFrame({
    "item": ["pen", "bag", "lamp"],
    "price": ["₹1,200", "₹85", "₹2,499"],
})

orders["price_num"] = (
    orders["price"]
    .str.replace("₹", "", regex=False)
    .str.replace(",", "", regex=False)
    .astype(float)
)

print(orders)
print("total:", orders["price_num"].sum())
print("mean :", round(orders["price_num"].mean(), 2))
```

Each `.str.replace(old, new, regex=False)` deletes one offending character: first
the `₹` symbol, then the thousands comma, leaving a clean numeric string like
`"1200"`. Only then does `.astype(float)` succeed, turning `"1200"` into `1200.0`.
With a real numeric column, `.sum()` and `.mean()` work as expected — the total
comes to **3784.0**, which would have been impossible on the original text. Passing
`regex=False` tells pandas to treat the pattern as a literal string, which is the
safe choice when removing punctuation that could otherwise be read as a regular
expression.

The same recipe handles units and other currencies: drop a `"$"` the same way, or
strip a trailing `" kg"` before converting. The shape never changes — **remove what
isn't part of the number, then `astype` the rest** — and a column you couldn't do
maths on becomes one you can.

A quick word on dates, the third common offender: they arrive as `"2026-06-29"`,
`"29/06/2026"`, and `"June 29, 2026"`, all text. Pandas has a dedicated tool for
them, `pd.to_datetime`, which parses many formats into a real date type. The
principle is the same one running through this whole chapter — a value that *looks*
like a date or a number is still text until you convert it.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. Pandas compares text **exactly**, so `"  Male"`, `"male"`, and `"M"` are
   different values — case, whitespace, and abbreviations split one real category
   into many.
2. `.str.strip()` removes stray whitespace and `.str.lower()` forces one case;
   chaining them (`.str.strip().str.lower()`) normalises a whole column at once.
3. To fold synonyms together, **normalise first, then map** with
   `.replace({...})` — a small dictionary of clean spellings to one label.
4. A number wearing a symbol or comma (`"₹1,200"`) is text: strip the non-numeric
   characters with `.str.replace(..., regex=False)`, then `.astype(float)` before
   doing any maths.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.
The first two use tiny hand-made values so the answers are exact.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

Write `clean_text(s)` that normalises one string: trim the whitespace from both
ends and lowercase it. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_clean_text
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_clean_text
def clean_text(s):
    """Trim leading/trailing whitespace and lowercase the string."""
    return ____  # TODO: s.strip().lower()

run_tests([
    ("trims+lowers", clean_text("  Hello "), "hello"),
    ("already clean", clean_text("male"), "male"),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Python strings have the same two methods pandas uses on a column. `s.strip()`
removes the spaces at each end, and `.lower()` forces lowercase. Chain them on the
input: strip first, then lower.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def clean_text(s):
    return s.strip().lower()
```

`s.strip()` drops the leading and trailing spaces, turning `"  Hello "` into
`"Hello"`; `.lower()` then makes it `"hello"`. A string that is already clean, like
`"male"`, passes through unchanged. This is exactly what `.str.strip().str.lower()`
does to a whole column, one value at a time.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

Write `to_number(s)` that turns a price string into a `float`. Strip a leading
currency symbol (handle both `₹` and `$`) and any thousands commas, then convert
what's left. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_to_number
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_to_number
def to_number(s):
    """Strip a currency symbol and thousands commas, then return a float."""
    return ____  # TODO: float(s.replace("₹", "").replace("$", "").replace(",", ""))

run_tests([
    ("rupees", to_number("₹1,200"), 1200.0),
    ("plain", to_number("3.5"), 3.5),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Remove each unwanted character with `str.replace(old, "")`, which deletes every
copy of `old`. Chain three of them — one for `"₹"`, one for `"$"`, one for `","` —
so a value with none of them (like `"3.5"`) is untouched. Then wrap the result in
`float(...)`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def to_number(s):
    return float(s.replace("₹", "").replace("$", "").replace(",", ""))
```

Each `.replace(old, "")` strips one character: `"₹1,200"` loses its `₹` to become
`"1,200"`, then its comma to become `"1200"`, which `float(...)` reads as `1200.0`.
A plain `"3.5"` has none of those characters, so the three replaces leave it alone
and `float(...)` returns `3.5`. This is the single-value version of the
`.str.replace(...).astype(float)` chain you used on a column.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Take a messy category column such
as `["M", "male", " Male "]`, normalise it (strip + lowercase + map to one label),
and report the **unique values before and after** so the consolidation is visible.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Builds a Series from a messy category column and shows its unique values *before*
  cleaning (three or more variants).
- Normalises with `.str.strip().str.lower()` and folds abbreviations together with
  `.replace({...})`.
- Reports the unique values *after*, showing they collapse to the single intended
  label.

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`series.unique()` lists the distinct values, so print it once before and once after
cleaning. Between the two prints, chain `.str.strip().str.lower()` and then
`.replace({"m": "male"})` to fold the abbreviation into the full word.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

messy = pd.Series(["M", "male", " Male "])
print("before:", list(messy.unique()))

cleaned = messy.str.strip().str.lower().replace({"m": "male"})
print("after :", list(cleaned.unique()))
```

Before cleaning, `unique()` shows three variants — `['M', 'male', ' Male ']` —
because case and the stray spaces make them distinct. After
`.str.strip().str.lower()` they all become `"male"`, and `.replace({"m": "male"})`
folds the abbreviation in, so `after` prints the single label `['male']`: one real
category, counted once.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch14.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

Real datasets rarely arrive tidy. With Chapter 4's missing-value and type tools and
this chapter's string toolkit, you can take a column written by many hands —
inconsistent case, stray spaces, currency symbols, abbreviations — and turn it into
clean text and real numbers your analysis can trust. Next we move from cleaning
single columns to combining whole tables: joining datasets together and grouping
rows to summarise them.
